content.assessment: This proposal leaves tracking age and comprehensive-provision structure entirely unchanged, instead pursuing compensatory funding and quality-levelling. The Finnish lens, whose central finding is that structural design (tracking age, comprehensive provision) drives the between-school variance outcome, would view this as addressing symptoms rather than the structural driver identified as most consequential. Finland's own high early-PISA performance was attributed specifically to the elimination of the dual-track structure rather than to compensatory funding alone within a still-tracked system. However, Finland's later erosion via informal 'emphasized classes' and school-choice segregation also shows that structural reform alone is not self-sustaining, meaning some forms of ongoing compensatory or quality investment might be a plausible complement, if not substitute, to structural reform.
opportunities: Could function as a necessary complement to structural reform, given Finland's own recent difficulties show that structural comprehensiveness alone does not guarantee sustained equity or performance (F-live-finnish_reform-07)
strengths: Targeted compensatory investment addresses within-school and between-school resourcing gaps that could complement, though not substitute for, structural reform

Recognizes that informal/within-school sorting persists even after formal structural change, an insight consistent with Finland's own experience of persistent informal segregation (F-live-finnish_reform-08)
threats: Without structural change, targeted funding could be systematically offset by continued sorting into and out of selective tracks, similar to how Finnish informal sorting mechanisms have partially offset formal comprehensive design (F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09)
weaknesses: Does not alter tracking age or comprehensive provision, the two dimensions the Finnish case identifies as most strongly linked to reduced between-school inequality (F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Cross-national evidence indicates early tracking itself, independent of funding levels, is linked to greater social-background impact on achievement, a mechanism this proposal does not address (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
